title: Seasons of Business: Navigating Growth, Systems, and Cash Flow
summary: Erin opened with a framework comparing business cycles to the four seasons—winter, spring, summer, and fall—encouraging participants to recognize which season they are in and act accordingly. The session transitioned into an open Q&A where participants shared their current business stages, discussing challenges around systems-building, team transitions, cash flow management, and hiring. Practical resources for fractional CFOs, bookkeeping, and financial planning were also exchanged among the group.

teachings.explanation: Business cycles can be mapped to the four seasons: winter is painful and grueling but necessary; spring brings new growth and excitement after hardship; summer is fun, active, and expansive; fall is beautiful but carries the awareness that winter is coming. Recognizing which season you are in helps you know what actions to take and what to anticipate next.
teachings.quote: It's important to recognize what these are and then what to do with them.

teachings.explanation: No business season lasts forever, whether it is a difficult winter or an exciting summer. Acknowledging the temporary nature of each phase helps business owners persist through hardship and stay grounded during success.
teachings.quote: It's a phase, it's a season. So you got to stick with it and you got to do what's in front of you.

teachings.concept: Vertical Brick Stacking vs. Horizontal Foundation
teachings.explanation: Some businesses grow quickly by stacking decisions vertically—like bricks stood on end—which is unsustainable at scale. Around years 5–7, many owners must pause, restructure their foundation horizontally, and build systems that can support a much taller structure going forward.
teachings.quote: If you want to build a 10 story building, you line the bricks out like a house, but some people start with the bricks the other way... and they get to a point, year five or six, where this thing is not sustainable.

teachings.explanation: The period between years seven and ten is historically a winter phase focused on building systems, aligning personnel, and restructuring the business so it can sustain further growth. It feels like a grind but is essential preparation for the next spring.
teachings.quote: That seven to ten years historically is yeah, exactly. The winter of just making sure things are set up.

teachings.concept: Client-Facing Timeline as a Transition Tool
teachings.explanation: When clients are handed off between departments or team members, providing a simple visual timeline of the entire project flow keeps the focus on the client's experience rather than internal personnel changes. This reduces friction and builds confidence in the process.
teachings.quote: It becomes less about the people on your team and more about the client and how they're going to think through the flow. I've seen that work really well.

teachings.explanation: Business decisions about hiring should be driven by 12-month projections of signed work and potential work, with a clear view of monthly revenue per project and fixed expenses like payroll, taxes, and insurance. Looking 6–12 months ahead allows leaders to time hiring moves without overextending.
teachings.quote: We're always looking six months to a year out for how we're going to position our personnel.

teachings.explanation: In construction businesses with short project cycles, hiring often only happens when the owner can no longer withstand the workload alone. As project durations grow longer, it becomes easier to project cash flow and make hiring decisions earlier and more strategically.
teachings.quote: The first probably ten years, it always felt like I was so like, I didn't make a move until I was like, could no longer withstand it.

teachings.concept: Pricing for Value, Not Just to Win Work
teachings.explanation: Bidding jobs at discount rates to win clients often leads to lower-quality subcontractors, more mistakes, demanding clients, and eroded margins. When businesses shift to pricing at their true value, they attract better clients, use better trades, produce better work, and improve profitability even with fewer jobs.
teachings.quote: We just bid out the projects, what we deserve and what we are valued at. And we don't get all the jobs anymore and it's okay.

actionable_advice: Create a simple visual timeline document for clients that maps the entire project flow from start to finish, making transitions between team members or departments feel intentional rather than disjointed.
Track cash flow on a weekly or monthly basis, calculating average monthly revenue per active project by dividing total project fees by the number of project months.
Build a 12-month projection that includes both signed work and potential pipeline work, but make hiring and spending decisions only based on signed work.
Look 6–12 months into your cash flow projections to time personnel additions so you are not caught off guard by payroll obligations.
Consider hiring a fractional CFO or virtual bookkeeper to build financial models and maintain monthly snapshots without the cost of a full-time finance team.
Search your state's Society of CPAs to find licensed fractional CFOs or accountants familiar with your state's tax requirements.
Read 'Simple Numbers' by Michael Crabtree for a straightforward understanding of business financials, particularly relevant to construction company owners.
If you are in a remodeling business with seasonal slowdowns, consider adding longer-duration projects such as ADU builds or multi-family conversions to maintain team continuity through slower months.
Send clients a brief video update proactively to keep them informed and confident, especially when working alongside external teams like architects.
When transitioning to higher-value clients, be willing to decline work that does not meet your pricing or project-fit criteria, even when the pipeline feels thin.
client_challenges: A participant at year seven is building systems and processes to support a growing team and wants consistent client experience across sales, design, and build departments managed by different people.

A participant at year 14 is trying to migrate from remodeling to custom home building and worries that his systems-oriented team would struggle with the chaos that characterized his earlier custom home work.

A participant in early-stage business is preparing to hire W2 employees but is uncertain about timing given variable cash flow from design-build projects that have long design phases before revenue begins.
A participant doing remodeling work faces seasonal revenue dips in winter and struggles with the cycle of hiring during busy seasons and potentially losing staff during slow periods.
A participant asked how to handle client handoffs across multiple departments—sales, design, and build—without it feeling disjointed or confusing to the client.
Multiple participants asked where to find fractional CFOs and bookkeepers, specifically ones with construction industry experience.
A participant asked whether there is a playbook covering how to time hiring decisions, project stacking, and team scaling.
A participant asked about FFE template resources and whether the group could dedicate a future session to FF&E process and tools.
notable_quotes: On business seasons: 'It's a phase, it's a season. So you got to stick with it and you got to do what's in front of you.'

On sustainable growth structure: 'If you want to build a ten story building, you line the bricks out like a house, but some people start with the bricks the other way and they get to a point, year five or six, where this thing is not sustainable.'
On knowing when to change your pricing strategy: 'They know they just can't do it again. It's a natural thing. You just can't do it anymore. You gotta make a move.'
On methodical growth with a mature team: 'You're a grownup now and the moves are methodical. You have to think about the effect and anticipate the effect of what your next decision is.'

On networking and genuine curiosity: 'Following that curiosity is the only way I can get myself out there. It's a matter of getting in a place where I'm truly curious about something. And that's what gets me out there.'
On hiring timing in construction: 'The first probably ten years, it always felt like I didn't make a move until I was like, could no longer withstand it. That changes over time.'
